items.text: I felt an earnest and humble desire, and shall do till I die, to increase the stock of harmless cheerfulness.
items.context: Public dinner in Edinburgh honoring Dickens, presided over by Professor Wilson. Dickens was articulating his artistic purpose and philosophy.
items.publicReaction: The Edinburgh audience received him warmly, and this statement encapsulated what became known as his core literary mission throughout his career.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens, ed. K. J. Fielding; collected from 1880 Chatto and Windus edition

items.text: I was anxious to show that virtue may be found in the bye-ways of the world, that it is not incompatible with poverty.
items.context: At the Edinburgh dinner, responding to criticism about his decision to have Little Nell die in The Old Curiosity Shop, and defending his portrayal of virtue among the poor.
items.publicReaction: Dickens received letters of remonstrance especially from women readers about the death of Little Nell; he defended his artistic choice while affirming his commitment to portraying dignity among the poor.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens

items.text: Virtue shows quite as well in rags and patches, as she does in purple and fine linen.
items.context: Dinner given by young men of Boston, where Dickens was expressing his belief in the inherent dignity and moral worth of the poor during his first American tour.
items.publicReaction: Dickens' statements about the moral equality of all classes were noted in contemporary accounts, and he received expressions of gratitude from American readers for his advocacy.
items.source: Speeches of Charles Dickens (Project Gutenberg)

items.text: I would beg leave to whisper in your ears two words, International Copyrights.
items.context: At a speaking event during Dickens' first American tour, he was advocating for international copyright protection, as his works were being pirated in the United States without any payment to him.
items.publicReaction: Dickens' pleas for copyright were widely attacked by American newspapers and public figures as mercenary. Anonymous letters and verbal attacks came daily, with newspapers calling him a 'mere mercenary scoundrel' and 'no gentleman'.
items.source: Dickens's 1842 Reading Tour: Launching the Copyright Question in Tempestuous Seas, Victorian Web

items.text: I have the honour to be entrusted with a toast, the very mention of which will recommend itself to you...It is the health of our Chairman, and coupled with his name I have to propose the literature of Scotland - a literature which he has done much to render famous through the world.
items.context: At the Edinburgh dinner, Dickens was proposing a toast to Professor Wilson, the Chairman, and celebrating Scottish literature.
items.publicReaction: The toast was received with enthusiasm by the Edinburgh audience, affirming Dickens' respect for Scottish culture and his growing prominence.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens

items.text: I shall love while I have life her people, her hills, and her houses, and even the very stones of her streets.
items.context: Statement about Edinburgh at the public dinner honoring Dickens, expressing his affection for Scotland after his first visit.
items.publicReaction: This declaration of affection for Scotland became widely quoted and contributed to Dickens' reputation as a champion of regional identity.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens

items.text: Sanitary Reform must precede all other social remedies, and that even Education and Religion can do nothing where they are most needed, until the way is paved for their ministrations by Cleanliness and Decency.
items.context: At a Metropolitan Sanitary Association dinner, Dickens was advocating for public health reforms as foundational to all other social improvements, drawing on reports from public health officials.
items.publicReaction: The speech was noted as influential in the sanitary reform movement of the 1850s; Dickens' argument that cleanliness must precede morality was seen as radical by some but gained support from health reformers.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens; Metropolitan Sanitary Association Anniversary Banquet

items.text: the air from Gin Lane will be carried by an easterly wind into Mayfair
items.context: Dickens used this vivid metaphor in his sanitary reform speech to illustrate that disease and poor living conditions in poor neighborhoods directly affect wealthy areas, demonstrating the interconnectedness of society.
items.publicReaction: The image was memorable and often cited as an effective rhetorical argument for why sanitary reform benefited all classes.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens

items.text: The bottom of his garden joins the bottom of ours, and the wind that blows over the cottager's porch, sweeps also over the grounds of the nobleman.
items.context: At the Gardeners' Benevolent Institution dinner, Dickens was using gardening as a metaphor for shared humanity across class boundaries.
items.publicReaction: The speech was well-received as it made a case for supporting working gardeners through charitable means.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens

items.text: Any gardener who has subscribed to it for fifteen years, and conformed to the rules, may...be placed upon the pensioners' list without election, without canvass.
items.context: Dickens was explaining the Gardeners' Benevolent Institution's policy, emphasizing that pensions should be given as earned rights rather than charity appeals.
items.publicReaction: The emphasis on dignity and earned entitlement rather than pauperdom reflected contemporary debates about poor relief and mutual aid.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens

items.text: the House of Commons is so little changed...that governmental machinery required public oversight, stating the need to keep institutions 'awake when it happens to have taken too much Ministerial narcotic.'
items.context: At the Administrative Reform Association meeting at Drury Lane Theatre, Dickens criticized Parliament's slow progress and the government's lethargy during the Crimean War.
items.publicReaction: Lord Palmerston dismissed the speech as 'private theatricals at Drury Lane,' which provoked Dickens to respond with indignation. The speech became associated with the novel 'Little Dorrit' which he was writing at the time.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens; Administrative Reform Association speech

items.text: It was the best of times, it was the worst of times, it was the age of wisdom, it was the age of foolishness, it was the epoch of belief, it was the epoch of incredulity, it was the season of Light, it was the season of Darkness, it was the spring of hope, it was the winter of despair, we had everything before us, we had nothing before us, we were all going direct to Heaven, we were all going direct the other way—in short, the period was so far like the present period, that some of its noisiest authorities insisted on its being received, for good or for evil, in the superlative degree of comparison only.
items.context: The famous opening passage of 'A Tale of Two Cities,' published in 1859 as a serial novel. Dickens opens with paradoxes to set the historical period of the French Revolution while making it relevant to contemporary 1859 England.
items.publicReaction: The opening became one of the most famous literary openings in English literature; audiences of the time recognized Dickens' satirical critique of contemporary politics embedded in the historical setting.
items.source: A Tale of Two Cities by Charles Dickens

items.text: Every traveller has a home of his own, and he learns to appreciate it the more from his wandering.
items.context: At a Commercial Travellers' Schools Anniversary Dinner, Dickens was reflecting on the experience of traveling and maintaining connection to home.
items.publicReaction: The speech was well-received by the commercial travelers, who would have closely identified with this sentiment.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens

items.text: I am no stranger – and I say it with the deepest gratitude – to the warmth of Scottish hearts.
items.context: At the Glasgow Athenaeum opening ceremony, Dickens was expressing his affection and appreciation for Scotland after his visit to preside over the opening of a working men's educational institution.
items.publicReaction: The statement was received warmly by the nearly 3,000 people at Glasgow City Hall, and contributed to Dickens' reputation as a champion of working-class education.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens

items.text: I believe I shall never hear the name of this capital of Scotland without a thrill of gratitude and pleasure.
items.context: At Glasgow, expressing Dickens' deep emotional connection to Scotland formed during this visit.
items.publicReaction: This statement became part of Dickens' enduring association with Scotland and Scottish themes in his work.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens

items.text: To strive at all involves a victory achieved over sloth, inertness, and indifference.
items.context: At the Birmingham and Midland Institute prize distribution, Dickens was emphasizing that effort itself constitutes success, regardless of whether one wins formal recognition.
items.publicReaction: The statement was well-received as it validated the aspirations of working-class students in the educational institution.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens

items.text: From these garish lights I vanish now for evermore, with a heartfelt, grateful, respectful, and affectionate farewell.
items.context: Dickens' final farewell statement at his last public reading at St. James's Hall, after reading from 'A Christmas Carol' and 'The Pickwick Papers,' concluding approximately 15 years of public reading performances.
items.publicReaction: The audience responded with tremendous enthusiasm, waving hats and handkerchiefs. The farewell was profoundly moving to those present, as Dickens was visibly in declining health.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens

items.text: I close this episode in my life with feelings of very considerable pain.
items.context: At his farewell reading, Dickens was explaining why he was retiring from public readings despite their continued success, citing physical exhaustion from the demands of performance.
items.publicReaction: Dickens made clear that exhaustion, not lack of ability or audience favor, was driving his retirement. The statement emphasized the toll that years of public readings had taken on his health.
items.source: Speeches: Literary and Social by Charles Dickens

items.text: In this brief life of ours, it is sad to do almost anything for the last time. Ladies and gentlemen, I beg most earnestly, most gratefully, and most affectionately, to bid you, each and all, farewell.
items.context: At his final Boston performance during his second American reading tour, Dickens was bidding farewell to the American audience that had received him warmly at the beginning of his tour.
items.publicReaction: The farewell was deeply moving; Dickens emphasized the emotional weight of saying goodbye, acknowledging that his American reception had been the starting point of this final phase of his career.
items.source: Charles Dickens' Farewell Speeches in America 1868, Charles Dickens Page

items.text: My gracious and generous welcome in America, which can never be obliterated from my remembrance, began here. My departure begins here too: for I assure you that I have never until this moment really felt that I am going away.
items.context: Also from his final Boston appearance, Dickens was reflecting on the arc of his American visits from his first arrival in 1842 to this farewell in 1868.
items.publicReaction: The statement captured the emotional significance of America in Dickens' life and career, marking the end of an era.
items.source: Charles Dickens' Farewell Speeches in America 1868, Charles Dickens Page
